Baggage Handling Optimization

==PAGE:3==
Summary
The accelerated growth in air traffic has sparked interest in developing new tools for
scheduling in the transportation sector all over the world. CPH Airport is one of the
many airports that has to balance costs and high quality services to keep up with the
increasing traffic. Along with departure and crew scheduling, one very important aspect
is the efficient use of the available baggage factories. This report will analyse the current
situation at CPH, will formulate and solve the Flight to Chute Allocation Problem
(FCAP) and will assess the performance of this implementation throughout a series of
tests.
The scope of this thesis is to provide the actual implementation, using Python pro-
gramming language and PuLP module for integer programming, along with GUROBI
and CPLEX as solvers, and to provide an analysis of different scenarios. The paper
only studies the pure deterministic problem by presenting a new model for the baggage
handling problem. In this way, we attempt to show the complexity of the problem and
the ways in which it can be solved.
==PAGE:4==
ii
==PAGE:5==
Preface
This MSc Thesis was prepared at the department of Engineering Management at the
Technical University of Denmark, in fulfillment of the requirements for a M.Sc. degree in
both Engineering Management and Transport and Logistics, since the author students
are from these two departments. The thesis makes up for 30 ECTS points and was
written during the second semester of 2015-2016 study year. The thesis was prepared in
collaboration with Copenhagen Airport, Karina Christiansen being the contact person.
The thesis was supervised by Jesper Larsen from the Department of Management
Engineering.
Kongens Lyngby, June 25, 2016
Iuliana Maria Cergedean (s121107)

CHAPTER 1
Introduction
Founded in 1925, Copenhagen Airport Kastrup (CPH) is the largest and, by a consider-
able amount, the busiest airport for international travel airport in the Nordic countries,
with 26.6 million passengers in 2015 (+3.8% vs. 2014). It is also one of the oldest inter-
national airports in Europe. The two terminals (T2 and T3) handled, in 2015, 254,838
flights with an average of 72,905 passengers per day, 94% of the flights being interna-
tional. CPH recorded 37 days with over 90,000 passengers per day, the busiest day of
2015 being the 19th of July with 96,118 passengers. This shows the significant growth
from 2014 and indicates the fast growth rate that the airport should expect for the next
decade [Air].
CPH also follows the growing trend of airport digitalization reaching 77% digital
check-ins (mobile, internet, self-service). The website recorded over 9,700,000 visits in
2015 and the mobile app has more than 1,000,000 downloads [Air].
Considering the amount of traffic and types of aircraft serviced at small airports,
we can say that baggage handling, in their case, is a relatively simple matter. Most
small and mid-sized airports, with only about a dozen flights a day, can easily function
relying on the experience of the ground staff to manually allocate flights in the available
baggage sorting system. However, a number of factors make baggage handling at large
international airports, with more than 300 daily flights, a more complex process, which
can significantly influence both the costs and quality of services.
Large international and domestic airports handle notable quantities of checked in
and transfer baggage, creating schedule optimization problem.
The total volume of
baggage handled also pitches the risk of congestion, delays, handling, and control errors.
Moreover, in many cases the physical size and layout of the airport significantly increase
transport times to and from the aircraft, shortening the remaining time for actual sorting
and handling of baggage.
It is estimated that the average number of bags per passenger is 0.6, for which CPH
==PAGE:12==
2
1 Introduction
offers 24 self-service bag drops along with the traditional check-in counters. This is the
trend for most airports and airlines, as detailed in the 2015 Baggage Report published
by SITA, a company providing IT and telecommunication services to the air transport
industry [SIT]. New routes departing from CPH, including long-haul destinations us-
ing double decker aircrafts, make optimization of baggage handling a vastly discussed
problem.
Since passengers perceive the quality of an airport’s baggage handling system in
terms of reduced number of mishandled items and waiting times for check in and bag-
gage reclaim, there is a continuous pressure to keep improving the baggage handling
process. Given the highly technical nature of baggage handling systems, only focusing
on technological aspects is a mistake that should be avoided. As this approach concen-
trates on redesigning systems to solve bottlenecks and peak flows, the result can be a
system much larger and more complex than necessary (as was the case of Denver Inter-
national Airport Baggage Handling System [Con]). For this reason, a process view that
focuses on optimising the total process chain, and consequently preventing bottlenecks
is much more advisable.
Taking into account all the recent number growths and increased interest in modern-
izing the infrastructure and airport logistics, we emphasize the fact that CPH airport
is in a growth phase that is not expected to end soon. Optimization - “the science of
better” - can provide solutions to problems that occur along the way in a flexible and
scalable manner to balance customer (and partners) satisfaction and cost efficiency.
1.1
Report outline
The baggage handling system at CPH Airport plays a crucial role in keeping customers
(passengers) and partners (airline and ground handling companies) satisfied. It can also
can make a huge difference in the airport’s ability to attract or keep a major airline hub.
A hub is the airport that serves as the main connecting point through which many flights
from the same airline are routed, CPH being the airport hub of Scandinavian Airlines
(SAS).
The steady rate of passenger number increase, more rigid security standards, and
the ever present race for profitability are the main challenges that airport operators
and ground handlers face everyday. Innovative baggage handling systems are the keys
for efficient airport operations by increasing throughput and improving handling costs.
==PAGE:13==
1.1 Report outline
3
Statistics show that baggage mishandling costs the air transport industry around US$2.9
billion annually [SIT]. Efficient baggage handling systems not only reduce costs but, in
the long run, also contribute to better rates when renegotiating contracts with ground
handler companies.
In this thesis, we are looking at how CPH handles the average of 43,743 baggage
pieces per day [Air] generated by the departing flights. CPH uses three baggage factories
(BF) to store and sort checked-in and transfer bags, through a system composed of belts,
chutes, conveyors, containers, trained personnel, and information systems. The problem
we are trying to solve is to assign all the daily flights to the available chutes in the
baggage handling factory while satisfying all the operational requirements. The final
objective is to minimize the number of flights that need to be manually allocated while
also using the minimum amount of shared chutes, which are more costly to operate.
The details of our optimization problem are elaborated in chapter 2: Problem
description. This chapter provides an overview of the current situation at CPH along
with a detailed visualization of the data sets provided by our contact at the airport, in
context with the problem we are aiming to solve in the later chapters. We here start
the discussion on the focus points of this thesis and the first steps needed to construct
the mathematical model.
There are several theses and articles that tackle baggage handling optimization prob-
lems, solving methods, and scenarios. This constitutes the starting point for our analysis.
We gathered the most relevant ones for our particular situation and summarized some
of their approaches and conclusions in chapter 3: Literature review.
In chapter 4: Mathematical model, we introduce the parameters used for the
construction of the mathematical model, explaining how they are connected. We then
formulate a basic mathematical model that allocates flights to chutes at certain time
intervals. Finally, we expand this mathematical model for our problem by including
a range of constraints that also take into consideration more particular aspects, like
handler preferences, proximity of the allocated chutes and type of chutes required by
each flight.
In chapter 5: Implementation, we solve the previously formulated model using
the Python programming language with its PuLP module library and default MIP solver.
A short description of Python and PuLP is given in the beginning of this chapter. We
build here the final version of the model, describing the steps taken and the shortcomings
we encounter on the way. Different scenarios are run here to gather and compare results.
==PAGE:14==
4
1 Introduction
Next, chapter 6: Result analysis describes the scenarios with which we tested the
model and discusses the limitations and robustness of the model, looking at the events
that would affect the running of the system and how to overcome them. We also briefly
compare results from two different MIP solvers and discuss the factors that determine
the huge differences in terms of running times and how can they be improved.
We are looking at the possible future development of our model and discuss what we
think can improve it in chapter 7: Discussion. Here, we look at the shortcomings we
encountered in running our model, other ways in which the model can be modified to
achieve better results and how can it be expanded even more to build a valid commercial
solution. We include here other possible scenarios that could be tested with our model,
adding or modifying the existing constraints.
Finally, we summarize our findings in chapter 8: Conclusion.
==PAGE:15==
CHAPTER 2
Problem Description
Air traffic volume is increasing and CPH is no exception. During the writing of this
report, the airport registered, for the first time in its 91 years history, more than 100.000
passengers in just one day. This did not come as a major surprise for the management,
as in the previous years, traffic has been increasing by 1 million passengers each year.
This summer it is expected that there will be 33 days with passenger counts in the
95-100.000 range [Air].
Reports from the industry indicate that airports are improving their service level
in most aspects, with baggage handling being one of them. Fewer mishandled baggage
occurs in the transfer phase, 49% of total delayed bags [SIT], down by 61% since 2007.
Another 21% of mishandled bags (defined by the SITA report as delayed, damaged, or
pilfered) are a result of failures to load or loading errors. Another 19% are a result of
ticketing or tagging errors. It is therefore clear that better solutions still need to be
implemented.
The problem we aim to address through this thesis stems from the handling process
at the airport. If the load of the handling companies is constantly increasing, sufficient
operation time must be assigned for each flight. Airports have come to rely on systems
that can generate optimal assignments, following a set of predefined rules.
Seen from a wider perspective, this problem is best described by the interval schedul-
ing system (reservations). The activities are represented by the flights. Each of them
will occupy a resource for a given time period. Starting and termination times are deter-
mined by opening and closing of the check-in counters. Because these times are decided
by the airlines according to their goals, the handling companies cannot change them.
Thus, the reservations will not have any slack (start and completion times must equal
release and due dates). In such a system, it is possible that not all activities will be
scheduled and a decision making factor would have to complete the selection procedure,
with several objectives in mind (such as maximization of scheduled activities or uniform
==PAGE:16==
6
2 Problem Description
resource utilization). Other applications of this system can be found in hotel bookings
or car rentals. In the air transport industry, a very similar application that has been
extensively researched is the check-in counter allocation.
Intuition and experience are what managers rely on when solving such problems. The
resulting solutions are, for the most part, suboptimal because of inherent complexities.
In instances where obtaining the ”best” solution is of crucial importance, mathematical
techniques should be the preferred tool.
2.1
Baggage handling process
The baggage handling process in an airport concerns three types of passengers:
1. outgoing - passengers that are leaving from current airport;
2. transfer - passengers from connecting flights;
3. incoming - passengers that arrive at current airport.
The focus of this thesis is on the combination of outgoing and transfer baggage, as
these are fed into the same system. Outgoing passengers drop their bags off at check-in
counters or automated drop-off points. Transfer passengers, unless they have specified
otherwise, do not have access to their baggage in the transfer stage of the flight. It
is simply unloaded from their arriving flight and entered in the sorting system by the
ground handler. Once inside the system, all bags run through specific checks. After this,
they must end up on loading chutes assigned to the respective flights. One flight can
be assigned 1, 2, or 3 chutes in the case of CPH, depending on destination, expected
number of bags, and any additional requirements. The time for which a chute is assigned
to a flight is dictated by the size of the aircraft.
In the case of CPH, additional difficulties in the operation of the baggage factory
are generated by the presence of multiple ground handlers. Five handling companies
take care of the scheduled and charter flights at Copenhagen Airport: Aviator (AVT),
SAS Ground Handling (SGH), Menzies (MNZ), Copenhagen Flight Services (CFS), and
MoveAjet (however, our data did not include flights assigned to this handler). In ad-
dition there are several others companies which handle executive jets. All are separate
companies working with different equipment, strategies, and goals.
==PAGE:17==
2.2 Data description
7
Apart from this, there are the physical limitations of the system. Foremost, there is
the loading profile of a chute that can be low, medium, or high. Some flights (BULK
type) can only be served by low or medium chutes. Other flights (ULD type) can only
be assigned to medium or high chutes.
Baggage handling process occurs in advance of the actual departure time for any
flight. Narrow-body aircraft have less loading capacity, hence chutes assigned to such
flights only need to remain opened for 2 hours 10 minutes. Wide-body aircraft require
3 hours and 10 minutes for this process. To allow all bags to travel through the system,
check in counters close 40 minutes before departure (i.e. for one flight, no more bags are
fed into the system in the regular manner after this point). To ensure there is sufficient
time to reach the airplane on the tarmac with the loaded wagons, all chutes assigned
to a flight close 20 minutes before departure. In other words, a chute will open nearly
3 hours before the departure of narrow body plane and nearly 4 hours in the case of a
wide body plane.
Baggage belonging to passengers from connecting flights arrive in the system much
earlier than that of regular passengers (can be as early as 24h). However, CPH has an
Early Baggage Storage system in place. Such bags are sent to a large conveyor system
and only reenter the system when chutes open before departure of the connecting flight.
Thus, this aspect of the problem does not generate further complications to our model.
While this solution of chutes is not unique to CPH, other airports use one (or mul-
tiple) large sorting/picking stations in conjunction with one or more handlers. All bags
arrive from the check-in counters to a large conveyor belt and the personnel will pick
the correct ones for the flights they are assigned to based on the tags, then place them
on transport carts. We have not been provided by CPH with the motivation for the
current solution. We expect that a system that relies on machines to sort the bags is
more efficient and prone to fewer errors. Other advantages and disadvantages certainly
exist, but we assume it has most to do with historical reasons.
2.2
Data description
2.2.1
BF3 Layout
Figure 2.1 contains the layout of chutes as of 22nd June 2015, along with color coded
information regarding the type of loading available and handler. The arrows on the right
==PAGE:18==
8
2 Problem Description
side of the figure represent access paths in and out of the factory. The chute groups have
additional space between them, where only a limited number of handling vehicles are
allowed.
SGH, represented in light green, operates most of the chutes available (76 out of 112).
Of these, most are of the Medium loading type (i.e. can serve both BULK and ULD
flights), highlighted by a blue border box. SGH is the only handler that operates chutes
with High loading type which can only serve ULD flights. These are highlighted by a
red border box in the figure. The rest of the chutes are loading type Low, highlighted
with a green border box.
Aviator, represented in yellow, is the second handler by number of chutes (18 out of
112). Of these, 10 are Low and 8 are Medium, suggesting that AVT is geared better
towards handling BULK flights rather than ULD.
Menzies, the last handler included in our data set, operates 6 Low chutes and 4
Medium chutes, represented in blue.
The figure also includes Flexible chutes (referred to as FLX), represented in red.
These are all of Medium loading type. They have been fitted with equipment that all
handlers can operate as needed. This is, however, a costly solution, as each handler must
install their own equipment, that then remains unused for the most part of the day.
The chutes in grey boxes are designated as Too Late chutes. These can not be shared
(each handler has at least one per physical group of chutes). As revealed by the name,
these are used as a last resort, when baggage arrives late, as a result of either passenger
or system delays.
2.2.2
Traffic data analysis
This section details initial findings based on the Excel file with the 4 days of traffic. The
file contained information for each flight (departure time, flight code, handler, destina-
tion, transfer, load type), along with a time profile for the chute demand. Since this
was formatted as needed in everyday use at CPH, some data manipulation had to be
performed so only relevant flights would be included.
All flights operated by CFS were removed from the data set, because they are handled
in BF2, thus falling outside of the scope of our paper. Flights that had a value of 1 in the
field Removed were accordingly removed from the final data set. Table 2.1 summarizes
==PAGE:19==
2.2 Data description
9
Figure 2.1: Physical layout of Baggage Factory 3 at Copenhagen Airport.
==PAGE:20==
10
2 Problem Description
the result of the data manipulation process. The resulting files, separated by traffic
days, only contain 6 fields of information: FlightID, TimeIndex (constructed from the
departure time to represent the 5 minute intervals from midnight), Handler, Loading
Type, Chutes, and Plane Type (NB/WB).
Chute demands are given as integer numbers and indicate the number of chutes
required by each flight (1, 2, or 3). For the most part, flights from the data set have
consistent demand during the opening time of the chutes. However, for those observed
with varying chute demand, the maximum value was stored and used for calculations.
A special note needs to be made about flights that were presented with a chute
demand of 0.5. These are domestic flights, which is why they present an exception to
the rule of only one flight per chute. The low volume of bags for these flights leads
to two of them being allocated to the same chute, if departure times coincide. For an
added challenge to our model, these flights have been converted to a demand of 1 in the
calculations. This transformation should be kept in mind, as in just the Medium day,
flights with a demand of 0.5 make up 13% of total flights included.

Figure 2.2 compares the information on the resulting 4 days of traffic, from the
perspective of number of flights assigned to each handler. Reflecting the situation from
chute availability, SGH handles most of the flights in all days. AVT and MNZ are both
handling around 50 flights/day, with the exception of the Low day.
The following three graphs, Figures 2.3, 2.4, 2.5, show the demand of chutes for
each handler in the Very busy day, as well as the available chutes (represented by the
horizontal lines). All three handlers will experience a shortage of chutes at various peaks
throughout the day, hence the existence of the flexible chute(FLX). AVT is the most
affected one, with a maximum of 9 chutes that need to be allocated elsewhere. This
occurs mainly around 12:30. MNZ also suffers from this lack of capacity, but with a
different profile. The under capacity is more spread out through the day, starting early

number of flights, 201 considered here. However, the Medium day includes 363 flights,
more than any of the other days (351 for Busy and 297 for Very Busy). A summation
of the chute demand results in the same ordering: Medium first with 525, then Busy
with 511 and Very busy with 473. A possible explanation is included in Figure 2.6. The
Very busy day has more flights requesting 3 chutes. These are the WB flights which also
require longer opening times. However, these WB flights only make up between 5-10%
of the flights in all days. We assume this classification for the scope of this paper, but
feel a more differentiated approach is possible.
==PAGE:25==
CHAPTER 3
Literature Review
The research articles that form the basis for our research were selected through a series
of searches using DTU Library’s FindIt, which provided access to restricted journals, as
well as the Google Scholar search engine. Access to DTU theses on similar topics was
provided by our supervisor, Jesper Larsen.
The airport baggage handling procedure is described by Abdelghany et al. (2006)
[A A06]. Crew members pick bags that arrive at piers (parts of a large conveyor belt)
allocated to flights and load them onto transport carts. The pier allocation procedure is
classified as an Activity Selection Problem (ASP) and the algorithm is based on a greedy
selection strategy. The model stores start and end times for the service period of each
flight, along with two stacks of piers (available and in use). The procedure for a greedy
solution will iterate through the events stack. Each time a start event is encountered, a
pier from the available stack is moved in the ”in use” stack. Reversely, when an end event
is encountered, the allocated pier is moved back to the available stack. If there are no
available piers, then the flight is marked as “open”. The performance of the algorithm is
measured by the number of open flights, with fewer being better. The model is expanded
with additional constraints and procedures - service times of 1hr (small flights) and 2hrs
(large flights), preferred quads (areas of the sorting station) for flights, same pier should
be used for flights to same destination in the same day, reservation of pier for 1,2,3,
or more destinations. The model is applied on a sample of 355 flights and 78 piers(as
obtained from Chicago O’Hare airport in the US), making this model comparable in
size to ours. The model with no reservation on pier destination performs best. This
article provided a good starting point for the model, revealing the trade-offs between
constraints and service goals.
Van Dijk and van der Sluis (2006) [Nic06] paper uses Atkins et. al (2003) as
a starting point and proposes a further combined approach. Using simulation to decide
the minimal number of desks in order to reach a certain service level for each separate
flight and integer programming to minimize the total amount of desks and desk working
==PAGE:26==
16
3 Literature Review
hours, operating realistic constraints are introduced for solving the problem for real cases.
The paper proposes a new model for the check-in allocation problem, similar to some
notable combinatorial optimization models, showing the complexity of the problem and
using a real case study to present the computational results.
Axelsen (2013) [Axe13] focuses on a different aspect of the problem of assigning
baggage sorting chutes to schedule departures under a range of conditions. The key
element here is the Early Baggage Storage (EBS), which is a continuously circulating
conveyor belt that stores early checked in baggage until a chute is opened (in CPH,
a bag can be checked in 24 hours before departure). This EBS system has a limited
capacity and the thesis aims to find a solution for decreasing the load of the system. The
immediate attention falls on the timetable of the departure chutes, which determines
the load on the above mentioned system. In this case, the opening times (OT) and
closing times (CT) are mainly calculated based on the expected influx of bags, but the
handling companies also have specific requirements such as preferred chutes or timetables.
Therefore, if a flexible timetable is to be used, the model looks for the feasible solution
to decrease the maximum load of EBS system.
The problem is first described using a Simple Allocation Model (SAM) and further
expanded using a pattern generation approach, with allocations modelled as patterns.
GAMS is used to implement the models and to solve this problem with results claiming
a more efficient utilization of the baggage handling facility. The model used lead to the
solution of extended opening times, which decreased the maximum load level by 23%.
The Chute Scheduling Problem is introduced by Hafþórsdóttir and Bjarnadót-
tir (2014) [AÝ 13], modelling the situation of Copenhagen Airport. Multiple ground
handlers require access to a set of chutes instead of the usual single pick belt; sharing of
chutes is allowed. The model optimizes the allocation of these chutes according to a given
flight schedule and prerequisites of the ground handling companies. The initial problem
is expanded with a Pattern Problem (PP) that requires column generation in the case
of large airports with a high number of flights. The check-in counter allocation is used
as a building block for the model, along with procedures from manpower scheduling. In
order to make the model solvable by a MIP solver, it is transformed according to the
Cutting Stock Problem, by generating possible patterns. The mathematical model relies
on two reward matrices (requirements and preference) for allocating flights to chutes.
The goal is to maximize the total reward for all flights in one run. Using column
generation on all possible patterns, an algorithm is implemented in Java and solved with
==PAGE:27==
3 Literature Review
17
CPLEX. Data for four days of traffic was supplied by CPH, including a total number
of approximately 1100 flights. Several variations and solutions are presented for each
of the four days, with running times of 2-7 minutes. Only 2 flights are not assigned
chutes in the busiest day of traffic, while a number of chutes need to be shared between
handlers. When sharing is not allowed in the model, the number of unsatisfied flights
increases considerably for 3 out of the 4 days. Modified data sets are also tested, by
adding sub-sortation to some flights or doubling a random amount of flights, as well as
the available chutes.
Andersen et.
al (2015) [LMH13] also formulates a Chute Scheduling Problem
(CSP), considering the continuous growth of air travelling in the last few years and the
need of efficient use of baggage handling facilities. The paper focuses on the column
generation method to solve the CSP for several scenarios with different levels of activity.
Furthermore, like Axelsen (2013) [Axe13], the model takes into consideration the
possibility of having different operating times for a flight’s allocated chutes in order to
fulfill the requirement of separating the handlers. The paper is of particular interest for
the theoretical and implementation of column generation method, along with integration
of the different scenarios in solving the problem.
The results show that the new chute schedules, with varying opening times, meet
88% to 95.11% of the total demand, concluding that the capacity of the current baggage
handling facilities is acceptable to cope with the expected growth. However, the separa-
tion of the handling companies turned out to be difficult to enforce as a hard constraint.
Alternatively, a penalty system is proposed for borrowing chutes that increase with the
distance between the borrowed chute and the cross line in the shared area.
Ascó (2012) [A A12] proposes an evolutionary algorithm to solve the The Airport
Baggage Sorting Station Assignment Problem (ABSSP). Their solution builds on Ab-
delghany (2006) and several other algorithms initially created for the Airport Gate
Assignment Problem (AGAP, assigning gates for arriving and departing aircraft). We
consider this algorithm useful, even though the setup of this problem is different from
CPH (sorting is already performed, it is a question of only allocating the chute at the
right time), because the algorithm uses a dummy sorting station to which flights are
assigned, if they cannot be assigned to a real sorting station. This feature became useful
in our model from the first tests, when the flights that could not be allocated caused the
solver to output infeasible solutions.
The problem proposed by Barth et al.
(2013) is limited to just the transfer
==PAGE:28==
18
3 Literature Review
baggage (i.e. passengers that are just changing airplanes) [TC 13]. Their solution is
successfully implemented at Frankfurt Airport. The goal of the model is to optimize
the trips of containers or dollies from the parking positions of aircraft in relation to the
infeed stations, from where the bags are delivered to the sorting station. While in the
case of CPH there are not as many transfer flights as Frankfurt, we find it important
to observe the operation of this model as the basic structure is similar. And, since two
thirds of all luggage lost in the US is actually lost in the transfer phase (Scottberg,
2009) [E09], a good baggage handling system will aim to minimize the amount of lost
luggage. Constraints in the model arise from handling capacity of in-feed stations, limits
on number of flights served, available time for each piece of luggage, and balanced use of
the in-feed stations, to name just a few. A sample of approximately 23.000 flights was
supplied by the partner airport for analysis. As a result of this model being implemented,
only one human dispatcher is able to allocate all incoming flights with ease, as it only
takes a few seconds to generate a solution.
Though treating a slightly different problem, the optimization of airport check-in
service, Bruno and Genovese (2010) [G B12] article gives a good understanding
of the mathematical model and the constraints used to balance the operative costs of
service and the passenger waiting time at the terminal. The model takes into account
several economic objectives such as costs of operating a desk, additional staffing costs
for extra hours and personnel workload in peak hours. The results from the proposed
model were tested with satisfactory outcome, using a smaller set of data than in the
case of CPH.
==PAGE:29==
CHAPTER 4
Mathematical Model
Having described the current situation at CPH, as well as a theoretical background for
this problem, we will now focus on the mathematical programming formulation. This
will include the set of variables representing actions that can be taken in the process
of baggage handling in BF3. The problem is denoted as Flight to Chute Allocation
Problem (FCAP) and the objective is to produce a mathematical model that gives the
optimal daily schedule in BF3. The following model will attempt to optimize, in the
minimization sense, a function of these variables. This function gathers each possible set
of decisions into a penalty score that helps us assess the quality of each solution found.
The penalty does not have a tangible currency, since we were not presented with any
data regarding the costs involved in the baggage handling process. The limitations of
the model are represented by a set of constraints. Based on the data provided by CPH,
the allocation problem is subject to five important constraints:
1. Each flight has to be allocated to the required number of chutes. For example,
flight SK2880 requires 2 chutes. Allocating 1 chute to this flight is considered not
enough to have it efficiently serviced; allocating 3 chutes is an obvious waste of
resources (and raised costs).
2. Each flight has to be serviced by the required number of chutes for all the necessary
time intervals before departure. For example, flight SK2880 has the departure time
at 08:00 and is using a narrow-body aircraft. The two chutes assigned to service
this flight have to be opened for 2 hours and 10 minutes, from 05:30 until 07:40.
3. Each flight has to be allocated to a chute corresponding the handler it services.
For example, flight SK2880 is operated by SGH, therefore it has to be allocated
to two chutes serviced by SGH.
4. Each flight has to be allocated to a chute corresponding the type of load of the air-
craft. For example, flight SK2880 is a BULK type, therefore it has to be allocated
==PAGE:30==
20
4 Mathematical Model
to two chutes of low or medium profile.
5. Each flight has to be allocated to adjacent chutes. For example, flight SK2880
requiring two chutes cannot be allocated to chutes 22 and 96, or any other com-
bination of chutes that are serviced by the correct the handler, with the correct
loading profile, but are not physically located next to one another.
The problem shows compelling resemblance with some widely studied combinatorial
optimization models in a variety of areas ranging from management to computing. Some,
Van Dijk and van der Sluis (2006) [Nic06] and Abdelghany et al. (2006) [A
A06], constitute the base on which we build our mathematical model. Although the
problem is not a novelty in airport management, most of the articles considered for our
thesis concentrate on assigning flights to check-in counters, gates, and runways. All
these instances have a certain degree of similarity in structure; the specific constraints
of adjacency and time intervals from our model make for an equally complex problem
that deserves adequate research.
Besides providing a daily schedule with flights allocated to chutes, our model tries
to provide an allocation using the least possible number of flexible chutes while
respecting all of the above enumerated constraints. Flexible chutes are, as described in
chapter 2: Problem Description, an extra cost for CPH for both usage and maintenance.
The extra costs come from the equipment requirement which has to be used by all three
handlers. Because of these requirements, we also observe that flexible chutes occupy a
central position within the BF3 layout, facilitating access of all handlers.
CPH recognizes (and this is also supported by the data provided) that BF3 is now
used at almost maximum capacity during peak hours in the very busy days. Simply
minimizing the number of flexible chutes might not result in the best scheduling solutions.
Having this in mind and observing the apparently empty space in BF3 (see Figure 4.1),
which could possibly accommodate future chutes, we can make proposals for the layout
of this area based on the results we get from running our model on different scenarios.
Let us now describe the underlying mathematical model for our problem. To begin
constructing it, we used a previous approach named Simple Allocation Model (Axelsen,
2013) [Axe13], where the problem is simply assigning a number of daily flights to the
available chutes. The only decision variable here is xf,c, getting the value 1 if flight f is
allocated to chute c. The limitations of this model are quite obvious since it does not
take into account the adjacency of allocated chutes or any time constraints. This aspect
==PAGE:31==
4 Mathematical Model
21
Figure 4.1: Empty area in BF3 that might accommodate chutes in the future.
is essential as flights demand more than one chute and, in case of a demand of two or
three chutes, adjacent chutes are required. Therefore, we can say that the FCAP can be
formulated as a large integer programming problem.
In order to ensure the adjacency of the allocated chutes for a flight, we created
workstations. A workstation is a group of one, two, or three consecutive chutes, based
on the structure of BF3 provided by CPH Airport (see Figure 4.2). A more detailed
explanation about how these workstations are created and later used is given in chapter 5:
Implementation. In creating these workstations, we do not include the ”Too Late” chutes
and Early Baggage System (EBS), as these two fall outside the scope of our optimization
process.
The allocation model is built to easily match the different approaches and future
changes in the traffic data.
Having a crucial role in the model solving process, the
objective and the constraints are modelled in a way that makes it possible to quickly
change the focus of the model in order to keep up with the development of BF3. The way
Figure 4.2: Example of formation of workstations from consecutive chutes.
==PAGE:32==
22
4 Mathematical Model
that the problem is formulated and the specific constraints that have to be considered
make it hard to be decomposed into independent problems since the allocations overlap
across time intervals. Therefore, the minimum time window for which the model can
give a feasible solution is 24 hours.
The following summary of notation includes all sets, parameters and variables used
in the mathematical model.
Summary of notation
Sets
F
Flights
CH
Chutes
W

T

Parameters
Bf,i
Is 1 if flight f requires one or more chutes at time t
Cf,w
Variable penalty system
Dch,w
Is 1 if chute ch is part of workstation w
Decision variables
xf,w ∈{0, 1}
Is 1 if flight f is assigned to workstation w
yf,w ∈{0, 1}
Is 1 if flight f is unassigned
Table 4.1: Summary of notation table for our mathematical model.
Sets F, CH, W and T are generated from the data supplied by CPH Airport. Set
CH contains all the chutes corresponding to BF3, including flexible chutes and excluding
too late chutes. These are the scarce resource that need to be efficiently used by proper
assignment and scheduling. Set W contains all the workstations of 1, 2, or 3 adjacent
chutes of the same handler and profile. It also includes the workstations containing
dummy chutes (chutes that replace the y variable, further explained in the Implementa-
tion section). Set T represents the discrete planning horizon, containing all the 5 minute
time intervals from 00:00 to 23:55.
CH = {ch1, ch2, ..., ch112}
W = {w1, w2, ..., w321}
T = {t0, t1, ..., t287}
Set F has four versions, corresponding to four different traffic load: low, medium,
busy and very busy.
We are testing our model for all four versions across multiple
scenarios.

4 Mathematical Model
Objective function:
min:
∑
(f,w)∈C′
cf,w ∗xf,w + 1000 ∗yf,w
(4.1)
Subject to
∑
f∈F
w∈W
xf,w + yf,w = 1, ∀(f, w) ∈C′
(4.2)
∑
f∈F
w∈W
dch,w + bf,i + xf,w ≤1, ∀(f, w) ∈C′, ∀i ∈T, ∀ch ∈CH
(4.3)
xf,w ∈{0, 1}, ∀f ∈F, w ∈W
(4.4)
yf,w ∈{0, 1}, ∀f ∈F, w ∈W
(4.5)
The objective function (4.1) consists of a sum between allocated and unallocated
flights. Since the main objective is to allocate all the departures every day using as few
flexible chutes possible, the penalty of not allocating a flight is set to 1000. The different
penalties for allocating a flight to different chutes is controlled through C matrix, the
priority being to allocate high/low type chutes first, then medium type chutes and, lastly,
flexible chutes.
The model has a number of variables depending on the number of elements of C
matrix. We therefore use C′ to restrict the creation of infeasible variables and avoid
unjustifiably overloading the model. Thus, we create only the variables that correspond
to a value greater than 0 in C matrix. The number of constraints and variables also
depends on the number of flights.
Therefore, for each scenario tested and for each
different traffic day, we have a certain number of variables and constraints that influence
the running times of the model.
The first constraint in the model (4.2) ensures that every flight is allocated to a chute.
The dummy variable y is added in order to obtain a solution at any time, even if it is
impossible to allocate all flights to the required chutes. This represents a dummy work-
station to which flights can be assigned, if they cannot be assigned to a real workstation.
We are going to be referring to these allocations as ”unallocated” or ”cancelled” flights
throughout the report. This approach is similar to Ascó (2012) [A A12], where sorting
==PAGE:35==
4 Mathematical Model
25
station 0 represents a dummy sorting station to which flights are assigned if they cannot
be assigned to a real sorting station.
The second constraint (4.3) uses B and D matrix to ensure that only one chute is
used by a flight over the entire time interval required. The use of workstations (to which
chutes are connected through D matrix) ensures the adjacency of the allocated chutes,
while B matrix secures this workstation (with the containing chutes) on the whole time
block of a flight.
Lastly, the variable domains are defined in (4.4) and (4.5).
==PAGE:36==
26
==PAGE:37==
CHAPTER 5
Implementation
The choice of using Python/PuLP for solving the problem of baggage handling optimiza-
tion was based on two factors. First, the code readability and syntax of Python allowed
us to read, manipulate and use the provided data in considerably fewer lines of code than
in other languages, such as C++ or Java. Second, we found very few examples of large
optimization problems such as ours documented academically. Hence, we considered it
an added challenge to build a working model in a relatively new and less explored way.
5.1
Python and PuLP library
Python has quickly become an extensively used programming language. Many devel-
opers love it because of its code clarity, syntax simplicity and extensive support from
the on-line community. Besides the personal preferences towards this programming lan-
guage, the main two reasons why we choose to explore this level of optimization are
Python’s container objects (e.g. lists and dictionaries) and the fact that Python is a
dynamically typed language. The beauty of Python’s container objects is that they can
hold objects of any type, including numbers and lists. This was of utmost interest for
us because in our case, a flight had attached to its flight code information about depar-
ture time, handler, chute requirement and aircraft type. All these information could be
stored and used in the simplest way possible using Python dictionaries. Furthermore,
in Python, there is no need to ever declare anything. An assignment statement ties a
name to an object of any type. If a name is assigned to an object of one type, it may
later be reassigned to a different type of object. This is why many programmers refer
to Python as a dynamically typed language [Fer].
PuLP is an open source library for Python that offers means to describe mathe-
matical programs and, through its interfacing with different solvers (open source and
commercial), to solve optimization problems. PuLP works entirely within the syntax and
==PAGE:38==
28
5 Implementation
natural idioms of Python. It supplies objects representing optimization problems and
decision variables, and allows constraints to be expressed in a manner almost identical to
the original mathematical formulation [S M11]. Providing close code-to-math mapping
and great code readability, PuLP helped us to rapidly translate the mathematical model
and to easily update it for running different scenarios.
Interfacing with solvers
Many mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) solvers are now available for PuLP,
both commercial and open-source (e.g. CBC). The open-source solver, CBC, was the
default solver for our Python/PuLP implementation.
However, after expanding the
model we found that the running times for solving a problem with a large number of
constraints were high, so we began tryouts using the more advanced solvers: GUROBI
and CPLEX (academic licenses for these were available from DTU), which show the
fastest running times on different optimizations [GURa]. Tables with time comparison
results can be found in chapter 6: Result analysis and Appendix A. GUROBI is already
used by CPH for the Check-in Counter Assignment and Gate and Stand Assignment
parts of operation.
For this reason and the faster running times, we expect that a
solution from the same solver would be preferred by CPH management [GURb].
5.2
Model implementation
The standard observed practice when programming for optimization problems is to keep
the model part (the actual code) separated from the data part. It is then possible to
reuse the model on different data sets, as we expected would also be the case in our
analysis.
5.2.1
Data manipulation
The Python/PuLP implementation of the model is based on the Excel files provided
by CPH Airport, from which we extracted the necessary data into .csv files. The files
contain information on 4 days of traffic, as introduced in the Problem Description section.
Since these are flights that actually occurred, they constitute the minimum data set that
the model would have to be able to process.
==PAGE:39==
5.2 Model implementation
29
The Excel file containing the physical layout of chutes in BF3 was used as a starting
point to construct a .csv file containing the chute groups in physical proximity of each
other. As stated earlier, every flight has a predetermined chute demand determined by
the size of the aircraft (narrow or wide body).
Each group contains chutes that are adjacent, are operated by the same handler and
have the same loading type. In order to restrict the possibility of allocations that would
split a flight in different parts of BF3, the concept of workstations was implemented
(a group of 1, 2, or 3 adjacent chutes with the same loading type, operated by same
handler). Dummy chutes were added after the initial model began reporting infeasible
solutions. These dummy chutes represent the y decision variable from the mathematical
model. Flexible chute groups get ”FLX” as handler name, while groups containing the
dummy chutes get ”CAN” as reference name (see Figure 5.1). Workstations of FLX and
CAN chutes were created in the same manner to standard chutes.
Figure 5.1: Groups of chutes organized by handler and loading type.
Pandas library was used to transform results from Python output (PuLP dictionaries)
to a more readable format in Spyder IDE as well as Excel and .txt files.
The final
solutions are transformed into Excel files where allocations are shown as flight code
blocks arranged on an XY axis system, where X-axis represents the chutes and Y-axis
represents the time intervals (see Figure 5.5).
==PAGE:40==
30
5 Implementation

Listing 5.1: Construction of C matrix.
C matrix (see code in Listing 5.1) is in fact a Python dictionary (line 1) holding the
combination (tuple) of each flight with each workstation as a key. We initially make the
value of an entry equal to 0 (line 4). The variable ”step” (line 5) holds the difference
between the number of chutes a flight requires and the number of chutes the respective
workstation has.
We then check, on line 6, if the flight handler is the same as the
workstation handler and we set the penalty of 1 if the load type of the flight matches the
type of workstation and if the workstation has the required number of chutes. We set the
penalty 2 for medium type chutes to prioritize the use of high/low profile chutes. This is
not a specific requirement of CPH Airport but, taking into account the placement of the
chutes in BF3, we are trying to create a buffer zone between workstations, when possible.
This may prevent overcrowding of a specific area of BF3. Flexible type workstations get
a penalty of 200, again if they contain the necessary amount of chutes. The dummy
variable y from the mathematical model transforms here in a dummy workstation with
a penalty of 1000 for allocating a flight here. A partial result of C matrix generation is
shown in Figure 5.2.
==PAGE:41==
5.3 Parameter construction
31
Figure 5.2: C matrix graphical representation.

7
elif v[4] == "NB":
8
if t in range(v[0]-30, v[0]-3):
9
b[k, t] = 1
Listing 5.2: Construction of B matrix.
B matrix (see code in Listing 5.2) is also a Python dictionary holding the tuple of
each flight with each time interval as a key. After checking the load type of the aircraft
(”WB” for wide-body and ”NB” for narrow-body), we attribute the value of 1 for all
the corresponding combinations of flight and time interval, 39 (3h and 15m) for a wide
==PAGE:42==
32
5 Implementation
body and 27 (2h and 15m) for a narrow body. We introduce here a buffer interval of
5 minutes that could allow for system corrections when disruption occurs. The buffer
interval can be increased or discarded by modifying the chute closing time in this matrix
(e.g. v[0]-4 will cancel the buffer while v[0]-1 will insert a 15 minutes buffer). A longer
buffer interval could prevent errors occurring from mixing baggage from two flights using
the same chute one after another. The result of B matrix generation is shown in Figure
5.3.

1 d = {}
2 for ch in chutes:
3

4
if ch in v[2]:
5
d[k, ch] = 1
Listing 5.3: Construction of D matrix.
D matrix (see code in Listing 5.3) is simply connecting the chutes to the workstations
Figure 5.3: B matrix graphical representation.
==PAGE:43==
5.3 Parameter construction
33
containing them. Therefore, if a chute is in a workstation, the value assigned to the tuple
is 1. The result of D matrix generation is shown in Figure 5.4.
5.3.4 X Matrix
1 x = pulp.LpVariable.dicts('x', [(f, w) for (f, w) in c.keys() if c[(f, w)] >
0], 0, 1, LpBinary)
Listing 5.4: Construction of X matrix.
Just like the other three matrices created earlier, the decision variable x is a Python
dictionary with the tuple flight, workstation as key. However, since creating all the
possible combinations of these two variables would consume more time and memory, we
choose to create only the combinations that are feasible and possible, using the C matrix
as a reference point. As explained earlier, C matrix checks the handler, number of chutes
required, loading type, and size of aircraft, then stores nonzero values where there could
be a viable allocation. When we create x, we only create the flight/workstation tuples
that have a value greater than 0 in C matrix. For example, for the 201 flights from
the low traffic day and 321 workstations (normal, flexible and dummy), we create 64521
elements in C matrix (with values ∈{0, 1, 2, 200, 1000}). In X matrix however, only
11835 variables were created (as reported by the GUROBI log). By default, GUROBI
exploits the sparsity of numerical coefficients [GURa]. Our Python formulation brings
an added layer of improvement, that results in time and memory savings. A better
visualization of the resulting matrix is included in Figure 5.5.
All three constraint matrices are observed to be sparse, as is frequently the case in
linear programming. An exception to this is the section of CAN workstations in the C
matrix where all combinations are generated, because the model has to be able to cancel
any flight (Figure 5.2 contains an excerpt of the C matrix to demonstrate this sparsity).
Figure 5.4: D matrix graphical representation.
==PAGE:44==
34
5 Implementation
Figure 5.5: X matrix graphical representation.
5.4
Scenarios
Since our objective is to minimize the number of flexible chutes used in BF3, we devised
several scenarios to be tested with our model to find the best combination of constraint
relaxations. As described earlier, the C matrix is constructed in a manner that provides
an easy way to add or modify penalties, and therefore construct new scenarios.
The basic scenario is the one where flights are allocated to workstations respecting
all the constraints regarding handler, chute profile and number of chutes required, using
flexible chutes only when no other allocation is possible, with the possibility of having
unallocated flights. Our information from CPH is that if there is a case of unallocated
flights, these are manually fitted into the schedule. This means that having a low number
of non allocations is desired.
The second scenario analyses the possibility of allocating fewer chutes than a flight
requires. We tested two versions of this fewer chutes allocation scenario: one that
allows an allocation of a flight requiring 3 chutes to a workstation that contains only 2
==PAGE:45==
5.4 Scenarios
35
chutes, and another that, in addition, also allows an allocation of a flight requiring 2
chutes to a workstation that contains only 1 chute. No other modifications are introduced
with this scenario, compared to the basic scenario. However, in chapter 7: Discussion,
we will expand on this scenario with more ideas on how to restrict these allocations to
certain handlers or flight types.
Another scenario is one where flights can be allocated to the required number of
chutes and any handler. Though this chute sharing scenario is not a valid solution for
CPH at the moment, since handler companies use specific equipment for their respective
chutes, it is interesting to see which handlers lack the necessary chutes at which times
of the day. The results of running this scenario are also useful for our proposals in
chapter 7: Discussion.
In the last scenario we are looking at maximizing the usage of the flexible
chutes while also assigning fewer chutes than required. The objective of this scenario is
to try to have as few non allocated flights as possible (preferably, none at all). The idea
behind running this scenario is that it would be preferred to have a feasible schedule
using all the available resources (in other words, maximize resource utilization), rather
than having resources that do not get used at all.
The results, comparison, and conclusions of running all the scenarios are further
discussed in the next chapter 6: Result Analysis.
==PAGE:46==
36
==PAGE:47==
CHAPTER 6
Results Analysis
The baggage handling process represents one of the most important concerns for air-
port operation management because it reflects the ability to produce an efficient daily
schedule, given the variations in baggage numbers, human resource skills of the ground
handlers, as well as unexpected events. A high-quality schedule translates into shorter
waiting time for departure after boarding, less mishandled baggage, and more flexibility
when special circumstances arise. These conditions heavily impact the perception of the
passengers on airport standards. It therefore represents a key concern for the airport
and ground handling service providers.
In this chapter, we will detail the results of the testing performed on the proposed
model, for all the scenarios described in the previous section, applied to all four days of
traffic data. Results will be presented in a clear way using tables, graphs, and figures.
We will also present here examples of a resulting schedule for the very busy day traffic
profile, with a total number of 297 flights and a total demand of 473 chutes. All
other tables, schedules and computational details can be found in the Appendix section.
As mentioned earlier, the first tests were conducted with the default solver of PuLP,
CBC (free licence). However, after we started expanding the model we found the running
times unacceptable, so a decision was made to switch to GUROBI solver.
All the
scenarios presented in this chapter are tested on a MacBook Pro with 8GB of RAM
memory, using 64-bit Windows Operating System in Bootcamp and Spyder as IDE for
Python/PuLP. The system performance is assessed only by comparing objective values
and running times, the difference between various solvers being outside of the scope of
our thesis.
The LP model that PuLP produces was also run with CPLEX using DTU’s HPC
facilities. We observed no differences in the objective values or in actual allocations
between GUROBI and CPLEX. However, we noted unusual variations in running times
for CPLEX. These can be explained as a result of different solving algorithms imple-
==PAGE:48==
38
6 Results Analysis
mented by the developers, combined with the very different hardware capabilities. We
feel that analysing the differences in running times between the two solvers falls outside
of the scope of our paper, even more so as CPH is already using GUROBI for other
optimization problems. Tables with results in CPLEX can be found in Appendix A.
To better interpret the results, we first need an overview of the distribution of flights
and chute demand throughout the day (Figure 6.1).
It can be clearly observed the
demand peak during morning hours, from 06:00 until 10:00, and afternoon, from 14:00
until 16:00. Therefore, we will expect that the non allocated flights will be grouped
around these traffic peak intervals. We also observe that the ratio between number of
flights and number of demanded chutes is not constant, showing that there are more
flights requiring more than one chute from 08:00 until 16:00.
6.1
Scenario 1: Basic allocation
The basic scenario is a standard allocation of a list of scheduled flights to available chutes,
for all the demanded time intervals, while satisfying all the operational requirements
outlined in the previous chapters. Therefore, the allocations are made with respect to
handler, required number of chutes, and chute loading profile. The objective of this
scenario is to minimize the number of non allocated flights along with the number of
flexible chutes used, the first having the highest priority.
This scenario is the base
Figure 6.1: Overview of the total flights and chutes demand for the very busy day
traffic profile.
==PAGE:49==
6.1 Scenario 1: Basic allocation
39
on which we build all the other scenarios and the first indication that the model can
be modified in many other ways in order to find the best combination of constraint
relaxations for the optimal daily chutes schedule.
In this first scenario, we are using the most basic form of the C matrix, with the
following penalties:
• 1, if a flight is allocated to high/low profile chutes;
• 2, if a flight is allocated to medium profile chutes;
• 200, if a flight is allocated to flexible chutes;
• 1000, if a flight is not allocated.
From the results of running this scenario (found in Table A.1), we can observe that
problems occur in the busy and very busy day, with 4, and respectively 7 non allocated
flights. Moreover, from the detailed data we get as output after running the model
we can plot the number of flights with their corresponding handler that are using the
flexible chutes or are being non allocated (see Figure 6.2). Given the fact that this is
the most restricted version of the model, a relatively high number of unallocated flights
is a predictable result. Running the model without the dummy containers which hold
the non allocated chutes results in an infeasible status of the solver.

a traffic peak for all traffic profiles. The schedule resulted from testing this scenario has
a chute demand satisfaction rate of 96.19%.

Figure 6.2: Overview of non allocated flights and flights using flexible chutes on very
busy day.
Figure 6.3 shows the actual schedule produced by the test. Chutes are displayed
along the X-axis and the time intervals are displayed along the Y-axis. The use of flexible
chutes can be observed highlighted by the yellow colored column containing flight codes
allocated to these chutes. It can be easily observed that there are very few empty gaps
in this schedule, a clear indication that our model produces an output that efficiently
utilizes all chutes in BF3. The grey stripe to the right of the figure represents the dummy
chutes containing the non allocated flights. We highlight the time intervals of these non
allocations to indicate the space where these flights can be manually scheduled.
6.2
Scenario 2: Assignment of less chutes
than required
We have constructed this model using data from Bruno and Genovese (2010) [G B12]
and Parlar and Sharafali (2008) [M P08]. The authors of these two articles propose
optimization models for airport check-in desks using estimations on arrival patterns
based on data gathered from various airports. Therefore, we can consider that there
will be intervals with passenger arrival peaks and idle intervals with very low service
demand. With more passenger (and baggage) data, it is possible to accurately forecast
the baggage flow and observe which are the time intervals where each flight actually
needs all the handler required chutes.
Since the decision of chute requirements for each flight is made by the handling
company, considering all the past statistical data and adding the extra safety margin,
==PAGE:51==
6.2 Scenario 2: Assignment of less chutes than required
41
Figure 6.3: Resulting daily schedule for Scenario 1.
it is worth exploring the alternative of some flights being serviced by less chutes. We
can only assume that this would be the case of, for example, flights where most of the
business passengers travel without checked in baggage, as well as low cost flights that
charge extra for baggage. As a cautionary note, for a flight that requires three chutes, an
allocation of only two chutes is a decrease of 33.33% of capacity, whereas an assignment
of only one chute to a flight requesting two chutes means halving the capacity. It is
expected that such a solution is not desirable, but it is a valid step towards variable
chute requirements.
The option of assigning fewer chutes can create a lot of feasible solutions and, subse-
quently, efficient scheduling for CPH with more in-depth constraints based on detailed
data about the flights. We will extend the idea in the next chapter 7: Discussion as part
of future work and development of the model.
==PAGE:52==
42
6 Results Analysis
6.2.1
Version 1: Assignment of two chutes for flights
requiring three chutes
In the first version of this scenario, we are updating the initial C matrix with penalties
for assigning less chutes than a flight requires:
• 1 if a flight is allocated to high/low profile chutes;
• 10 if a flight requiring 3 chutes is allocated to only 2 high/low profile chutes;
• 2 if a flight is allocated to medium profile chutes;
• 20 if a flight requiring 3 chutes is allocated to only 2 medium profile chutes;
• 200 if a flight is allocated to flexible chutes;
• 1000 if a flight is not allocated.

We can already observe a decrease of the non allocated flights along with a higher
utilization of the flexible chutes. However, as observed in Figure 6.4, we again see that
==PAGE:53==
6.2 Scenario 2: Assignment of less chutes than required
43
AVT is the handler of all non allocated flights and it has the most flights using the
flexible chutes. There is only one flight serviced by SGH which gets an allocation of two
chutes instead of three, one flight serviced by AVT and two flights serviced by MNZ.
This result proves our initial findings from chapter 2: Problem Description, where AVT
and MNZ show significant shortage of chutes during the traffic peaks. The schedule for
this scenario can be seen in Figure 6.5. The 4 flights to which only 2 chutes are assigned
instead of 3 can be seen highlighted in red (outside of the yellow bands).
In this version of the scenario, a percentage of only 1.68% of the 297 flights are
not allocated, again all of them being assigned to AVT. The chute demand of the 5
non allocated flights is a total of 11 chutes, while only 4 flights requiring 3 chutes
are allocated to workstations containing 2 chutes. This means that the chute demand
satisfaction of this scenario is 96.83%. The higher rate of successful allocations from
running this scenario is a visible improvement from the previous result and a clear
indication that constraint relaxation will eventually result in a schedule with no non
allocated flights and maximum chute demand satisfaction.
Figure 6.5: Resulting daily schedule for Scenario 2.1.
==PAGE:54==

6 Results Analysis
6.2.2
Version 2: Assignment of one chute for flights
requiring two chutes
So far, we analyzed the results of allocating less chutes only to flights requiring 3 chutes.
As the articles above mentioned state, passenger arrival rate is a non-stationary process
that is influenced by the uncertainty in passenger behaviour. However, some arrival
patterns can be observed around the world. One example would be that passengers
tend to arrive late for flights that are scheduled early in the morning. Furthermore,
more passengers arrive in the second half of the check-in opening time than in the early
minutes. The growing trend for on-line and self service check-in, as well as self-service
baggage label printers and baggage drop belts, allows passengers to arrive later than
ever, sometimes just in the last minutes before gate closing. The introduction of these
new technologies has shifted the curve of arrivals, from the blue to the orange line in
Figure 6.6, pushing the peak closer to the departure time. Therefore, in order to make
this scenario a viable option for use, we would need the passenger arrival profile for each
flight, along with baggage data.
To run the second version of this scenario, we again update C matrix with penalties
for assigning less chutes than a flight requires, this time adding the possibility of assigning
only 1 chute for a flight demanding 2 chutes:
Figure 6.6: The shifting trend of arrival distribution.
==PAGE:55==
6.2 Scenario 2: Assignment of less chutes than required
45

• 10 if a flight requiring 3 chutes is allocated to only 2 high/low profile chutes;
• 30 if a flight requiring 3 chutes is allocated to only 1 high/low profile chute;
• 2 if a flight is allocated to medium profile chutes;
• 20 if a flight requiring 3 chutes is allocated to only 2 medium profile chutes;
• 40 if a flight requiring 2 chutes is allocated to only 1 medium profile chute;
• 200 if a flight is allocated to flexible chutes;
• 1000 if a flight is not allocated.

Indeed, we see only one non allocated flight and a considerable reduction of flexible

to workstations of only 2 chutes, but there are 16 flights which get half the required

to 95.14%, the lowest rate yet.

to 1 chute instead of the required 2 is very high. We can see that, as in the previous

shortage of chutes for this handler (see Figure 6.7).

6.3
Scenario 3: Assigning the requested
number of chutes to any handler
In this scenario, we again update the initial C matrix with penalties for chute sharing
as follows:
• 1 if a flight is allocated to high/low profile chutes;
• 50 if a flight is allocated to high/low profile chutes serviced by another handler;
• 2 if a flight is allocated to medium profile chutes;
• 60 if a flight is allocated to medium profile chutes serviced by another handler;
• 200 if a flight is allocated to flexible chutes;
• 1000 if a flight is not allocated.
This scenario was constructed and tested in order to gather more information about
which handlers experience chute shortages and to get an idea about how these shortages
can be solved by optimization on the current layout. The first three scenarios show
that AVT flights find it most difficult to find a suitable group of chutes in the busy
and very busy days; MNZ also experiences chute shortages to a lesser extent.
The
shared chutes scenario is also inspired by previous theses which challenged the traditional
handler separation in BF3 allowing flights to be allocated to any chutes, with a higher
preference on the ones serviced by the required handler. This scenario shows that a future
uniformization of handling equipment would overcome all the shortages experienced now,
improving the flexibility and scalability of the layout of BF3 and permitting it to quickly
==PAGE:57==
6.3 Scenario 3: Assigning the requested number of chutes to any handler
47
Figure 6.8: Resulting daily schedule for Scenario 2.2.
adapt to the continuous increase in air traffic. We will expand on this idea in the next
chapter, where we take into consideration more factors for this discussion.

==PAGE:58==

6 Results Analysis
Figure 6.9: Overview of flights sharing chutes on very busy day.
As expected, the results of this scenario, presented in 6.4, show no non allocated
flights and only two flights (handled by AVT) that are using flexible chutes (a total of
5 chutes). Most of the flights allocated to other handler chutes are AVT flights (16 out
of 22), 88% of them using high profile chutes serviced by SGH. Since both AVT and
MNZ lack high profile chutes, and our model being constructed to prioritize the use of
high/low profile chutes, it is clear that the solver will rather allocate these flights to
chutes handled by SGH. It is necessary to mention that sharing should only be possible
when a handler does not have demand for those particular chutes. This means that a
handler cannot borrow a chute that can be more efficiently used by the owning handler.
A graphical representation of chute sharing is found in Figure 6.9, where we show
which other handler chutes are used by each of the three handlers. The result again
shows an acute need of extra chutes for AVT, either as flexible chutes or reserved chutes.
The schedule for this scenario can be seen in Figure 6.10. The result shows no flexible
chutes usage and no non allocated flights, and a slightly more condensed schedule with
significantly fewer gaps. This is the only scenario with 100% chute demand satisfaction.
In this scenario, we do not prioritize the assignment of shared chutes in a specific area
or from a specific handler (for example, a restriction on AVT only borrowing chutes from
MNZ). Therefore, any flight can virtually be allocated in any available gap (respecting
the loading type profile of the chute). Also, since our workstations are based on chute
groups that do not overlap on two different handlers, it is not possible to have assigned
chutes of different handler for the same flight. This constraint restricts the result of
the model, but since equipment differences make chute sharing a complicated approach,
splitting a flight between two or three different handler chutes is inadvisable.
==PAGE:59==
6.4 Scenario 4: Maximum flexible chute utilization
49
Figure 6.10: Resulting daily schedule for Scenario 3.
6.4
Scenario 4: Maximum flexible chute
utilization
After running all the above presented scenarios on all four days of traffic and analysing
the results, we came to the conclusion that flexible chutes are essential for proper schedul-
ing in BF3, with the current layout. Also, since sharing chutes is not a solution applicable
in real life, for the time being, we turned our attention on combining maximum usage of
the flexible chutes with the possibility of allocating less chutes than required. However,
since the model should prioritize allocations of the correct number of chutes in order
to avoid increasing the workload of certain handlers, we modified the penalty system
represented by C matrix as follows:
• 1 if a flight is allocated to high/low profile chutes;
• 100 if a flight requiring three chutes is allocated to only two high/low profile chutes;
• 150 if a flight requiring two chutes is allocated to only one high/low profile chute;
==PAGE:60==
50
6 Results Analysis
• 2 if a flight is allocated to medium profile chutes;
• 200 if a flight requiring three chutes is allocated to only two medium profile chutes;
• 250 if a flight requiring two chutes is allocated to only one medium profile chute;
• 50 if a flight is allocated to flexible chutes;
• 1000 if a flight is not allocated.
In this scenario, we lower the penalty for allocations to flexible chutes from 200 to
50 and we increase the penalty for allocating less chutes, making the choice of allocating
fewer chutes less desirable than using flexible chutes. We still keep the same system of
prioritizing the use of high/low profile chutes to avoid overcrowding in the central area
of BF3, where all the medium profile chutes are located.

The results of running this scenario seem to be the best solution we found so far.

==PAGE:61==
6.5 Results statistics

busy day. The flexible chutes are used at maximum capacity during peak hours and
a considerable smaller number of flights get fewer chutes than requested. A graphical
representation can be found in Figure 6.11.
The final resulting schedule for this scenario, found in Figure 6.12, shows a less
condensed arrangement than the previous scenario, with noticeably more gaps. The
only non allocated flight belongs to AVT and requires 3 chutes.
The non allocated
flights, along with the 10 flights that are allocated to workstations with less chutes than
required, give a chute demand satisfaction of 97.26%, the second best rate after the
chute sharing scenario.
6.5
Results statistics
All the results from testing the above scenarios show a chute demand satisfaction between
95% and 100%, meaning that the model needs minimal human interaction. They also
show that, by allowing handlers to share their chutes and by allowing a more flexible
division of chute among handlers, there is significant potential for improvement of chute
demand satisfaction, as well as system scalability for future traffic growth. As observed
in table 6.6, the current maximum chute utilization does not exceed 70%, with an average
utilization of around 40%. The standard deviation values of 11-12% point to a relatively
balanced use of the chutes, but with room for improvement. However, this statistics is
calculated for the entire period of 24 hours and, as we can see from the traffic data, there
is no traffic between 00:00 and 06:00. We can also observe that SGH has the maximum
utilization rate for all the tested scenarios. This is a predictable result given the fact
that SGH has the highest number of flights serviced and the most chutes in BF3. A
surprising finding is a minimum utilization for chutes handled by AVT. This can be
explained by the fact that AVT mainly handles flights that require a different loading
type chute than the ones they own. Therefore, AVT is forced to service these flights
using flexible chutes or, in the case of our different scenarios, to borrow, assign less or
not allocate any chutes.

Figure 6.12: Resulting daily schedule for Scenario 4.

Table 6.6: Chute usage statistics.
Table 6.7 shows a comparison of number of variables and corresponding running times
for the tested scenarios. As explained in chapter 5: Implementation, the key element
of our model is the penalties matrix C, which enables the model to output different
results with each added or deleted penalty condition. Since this matrix is the reference
point for the x variable construction, the more penalties we introduce (and implicitly
constraint relaxations), the number of variables increases. This had a direct effect on
running times, with a maximum of 3127 seconds for running scenario 3 on the very busy
day traffic profile. This is the first indication that, if we want our model to work for
airports with considerably more traffic (or modified for other types of optimization), it
==PAGE:63==
6.6 Robustness
53
needs to be restructured to further reduce the number of variables created.

6.6
Robustness
The final tests are constructed to give a perspective of the future, showing how the
potential growth in air traffic at CPH will influence our optimization model. We are
also investigating here how the model can cope with a higher number of variables. Since
air traffic is expected to continually increase in the coming years, it is important to know
how much load BF3 can handle before it starts to noticeably affect the chute demand
satisfaction.
We decided to run the scenarios with increased traffic on the model used for scenario
4, which gave the best chute demand satisfaction rate without allowing chute sharing.
However, since the model allows assigning fewer chutes than demanded, it is unrealistic
to increase the number of flights without somehow limiting this allowance. Therefore,
we modify the C matrix by increasing the penalties for allocations of less chutes, as
follows:
• 1 if a flight is allocated to high/low profile chutes;
• 500 if a flight requiring three chutes is allocated to only two high/low profile chutes;
• 900 if a flight requiring two chutes is allocated to only one high/low profile chute;
• 2 if a flight is allocated to medium profile chutes;
• 550 if a flight requiring three chutes is allocated to only two medium profile chutes;
• 950 if a flight requiring two chutes is allocated to only one medium profile chute;
• 50 if a flight is allocated to flexible chutes;
• 1000 if a flight is not allocated.
To make this test as relevant as possible and to compare the results with the previous
scenarios, it was decided to run the model for the very busy day profile. In the first
==PAGE:64==
54
6 Results Analysis
version, we increase the number of flights by 10%, adding 30 randomly generated flight
codes to the initial 297 flights. The added flights do not change the handler assignment
ratio of roughly 70% SGH, 17% AVT and 13% MNZ. We set random chute requirements
of 1, 2, or 3 chutes and random departure times.
Since we knew that the running times will certainly rise, we decided to limit the solver
to 4% optimality gap, based on observations from previous scenarios. The resulting
schedule from Figure 6.13 shows the 30 flights added (highlighted in blue) and the 31
flights using less or flexible chutes, or being non allocated (highlighted in red). The
chute demand satisfaction is 95.92%, a slight drop from the reference scenario 4, but
still a very good result. We observed insignificant raise in overall chute utilization.
In the second version, we increased the number of flights by 20%, adding 60 randomly
generated flight codes to the initial 297 flights, maintaining the same handler ratio. The
resulting schedule from Figure 6.14 shows the 60 flights added (highlighted in blue) and
the 37 flights using less or flexible chutes, or being non allocated (highlighted in red).
The chute demand satisfaction is 95.33%, (a slight drop from the scenario with only 10%
traffic increase and still a very good result). We also did not observe any significant raise
in overall chute utilization.
Table 6.8 shows a side-by-side comparison of the results from running both robustness
scenarios. Unsurprisingly, the more flights we add the more variables we create and,
therefore, we increase the running times.
However, there is a very big difference of
running times between these two scenarios and the explanation could be that the last
scenario has reached a built in limit of either the GUROBI solver or the hardware used
for testing.
