extra traffic. We gather all the chute demand satisfaction rate in table 6.9 and we observe
that our model managed to output results of minimum 95% for all the tested instances.
These rates show that BF3 is perfectly capable of handling the current traffic growth
rate, and will possibly not be affected by temporary disruptions (such as equipment
failure).

==PAGE:67==
CHAPTER 7
Discussion
A successful baggage-handling system is considered a system that manages to balance
handling times of bags and the costs involved in the process. In other words, a baggage
has to pass through all the check points in the system in the same amount of time
that the passenger does, from the drop-off point to the boarding point [Sch]. If the
system is slow, inevitable departure delays will occur or bags will never make it on
their flight. Our optimization model offers a tool to perform dynamic testing on various
scenarios and traffic loads providing insight in the overall layout performance during
different operational modes.
We are also trying to pinpoint bottlenecks and layout
design problems that can be addressed in a future expansion of BF3.
Our findings reveal a real shortage of chutes for both Aviator and Menzies, two of
the 3 main handlers at CPH, for all traffic days and throughout the operational hours.
The use of flexible chutes, which is the scope of our thesis, is crucial at the moment;
the first solution for minimizing their use would be allocating more chutes to Aviator.
However, we can only make proposals and assumptions on future projects of CPH. The
starting point of our discussion is the apparently empty area in the top left corner of
BF3 (as seen in the layout section), which, from our estimation, could accommodate
14 extra chutes. Assuming that, in the future, this area would indeed be serving the
existent baggage handling system, we can debate on which kind of chutes it should
contain. Flexible chutes certainly offer more flexibility for all handlers, but the costs
of necessary equipment might be an issue. Based on the fact that in roughly 80% of
the cases of flights using flexible or shared chutes, or being allocated fewer chutes than
required, the handler was Aviator, a future layout of this area could reserve 11 chutes to
Aviator and 3 to Menzies. This way, the extra costs of equipment are avoided and the
solution will partially solve bottlenecks and peak flows.
However, since air traffic is steadily increasing, more simulations need to be done to
show what would happen when the current layout of BF3 cannot cope with the situation
(proving that, perhaps, a complete repositioning is necessary). We therefore introduce
==PAGE:68==
58
7 Discussion
here the idea of uniformization of equipment. Optimization of the baggage handling
process should also mean standardization of the equipment used in order to create a
scalable and sustainable solution.
However, we are well aware that this requires all
stake holders in the baggage handling system chain to cooperate and share information
and costs, along with achieving their own targets.
New and innovative technologies
are starting to make things easier for baggage handlers; many companies, such as the
German company Siemens, the French group Alstef Automation, and the Crisplant
system by the BeumerGroup from Denmark are already using them for state-of-the-art
automation [Sch]. Smart Bag Tags and RFID, combined with powerful new applications
may replace the conventional equipment and pave the way for standardization at CPH.
These new technologies can also contribute to a decrease of servicing times in the
future. The chute opening times for flights are, in the present case, longer than what
we observed in subject specific literature. In Abdelghany et al. (2006), we found a
simpler classification of flights: small flights with 1 hour service period and large flights
with 2 hours service periods. This model uses a much smaller setup of sorting stations,
but it is an interesting aspect to research in terms of reducing service times for flights
that could permit it. As mentioned previously, in this thesis we do not have detailed
information about the type of flights, but many business, domestic or short distance
flights might be serviced in less time, since the number of bags is smaller than in the
case of long distance international flights.
This makes way for other possible scenarios focusing on this type of flights. The
first scenario, where we assume some flights can be serviced in less time is inspired by
Axelsen (2013) thesis, where extended opening times are used in the optimization.
However, our model takes the opposite side of this approach.
Another scenario could be a different method of chute sharing. More precisely, the
domestic flights that require 0.5 chutes in the initial data provided by CPH airport
(which we rounded up to 1 in our model) and have close departure times, can be grouped
together to use only one chute.
Also, taking into consideration that the number of
baggage arriving at check in has a peak in the last hour before the departure time,
another option could be overlapping the first few time intervals of opening chute time
of a flight requiring three chutes with the last time intervals of opening chute time of a
flight requiring one or two chutes.
As mentioned several times through the project, allocating fewer chutes than required
is not a particularly desirable solution, especially for flights that require two chutes to
==PAGE:69==
7.1 Further work
59
be serviced. However, our solution is meant to offer a daily schedule in a dynamical way
making it possible to use real time data as input. Therefore, it should be possible to use
the real time baggage data to make decisions on allocating fewer chutes where possible.
Another discussion point in the choice of solvers. We observed both CPLEX and
GUROBI to take a long time on the last few % points on the optimal solution. For
example, when running Scenario 3 on the very busy day, GUROBI found the objective
function value 1553 at 4.81% gap in 293 seconds and 1543 at 0.10% gap in 2476 seconds.
Similar behaviour was observed for running the model for the busy day (which took 3033
seconds) and in CPLEX tests. Lowering the optimality gap for the same set of tests
could dramatically decrease the running times while producing similar objective values.
Furthermore, the model should also be tested on additional solvers for result compari-
son in terms of objective values and running times. This is in line with recommendations
from GUROBI: ”Computational benchmarks across a range of models show wide perfor-
mance and robustness variations between different implementations. For example, the
open-source simplex solvers CLP and GLPK are on average a factor of 2.5 and 58 times
slower than the Gurobi simplex solver, respectively.” [GURa].
7.1
Further work
One possible subject of further research is the modifying the buffer time between depar-
tures using the same chute. A buffer could mean a service time for a chute after it has
been used so two flights should not use the same chute right after each other. We have
no information from CPH Airport about chute service times (if they are used or might
be useful), but we added an extra time interval (5 minutes) to the chute opening interval
to cover this buffer. However, since this is not a key point for our paper it should be
regarded as an extra feature that can be easily discarded. This option needs further
investigation to determine how much stress it puts on the system and if it adds up to a
lot of idle system time. First, more information is needed from CPH to clarify if there
are any buffer times in place now for chute maintenance, and, if they exist, if they vary
based on the flight that has just been served.
Another possible subject for further work might be allocations that also take into
account the destination or airline company.
For example, it could be interesting to
investigate the impact on the system if flights from the same company departing to the
same destination should always be allocated to the same chutes (or at least the same
==PAGE:70==
60
7 Discussion
approximate area of BF3). This approach could be welcomed by handling companies
because it would enable them to set specific flight tasks for chutes that are always serving
that particular flight. Also, this setup will allow any delayed baggage to be loaded on
the next flight to the same destination without reintroducing it through the system to
reach a further chute.
Furthermore, the model could be expanded to integrate the actual (or statistically
estimated) number of baggage for each flight. This addition will help adjust the number
of chute demand in accordance with the real-time data and provide a more efficient
schedule eliminating idle times and bottlenecks at peak hours. Also, delays of flights
were not taken into account as a part of the model. It is however an important factor to
include that would make the model even more scalable and realistic. Delaying a flight
could also be included as an option for non allocated flights, in the sense that the model
could push forwards the departure time to make room for flights.
Because the whole process of building this model was also a learning process, there is
much to be done in terms of optimizing the implementation code. We are well aware now
that there are a lot of better ways in which the constraints and variables can be built
that would reduce the running times of parts not concerned with the actual optimization.
A weakness that we found in the last tests of our model is that we create a very large
number of constraints that can be reduced. After the actual solve phase of the model,
the constraint creation phase is the second largest time consumer. This was the moment
we discovered that there are GUROBI built-in select function for tuplelist data types
that would have made a big difference. However, we began building the model using the
default Python/PuLP solver and in order to use GUROBI built-in functions we would
have needed to rewrite all the model code in GUROBI style. Nonetheless, better data
and code structure can make way for a commercial implementation of the model, with
a built in interface for fast parameter update and real time data use.
==PAGE:71==
CHAPTER 8
Conclusion
This thesis investigates solutions for optimization problems of flights allocation to chutes
within baggage factory 3 at Copenhagen Airport, focusing on the use of shared chutes.
The accelerated air traffic growth has underlined an acute need for upgrading the baggage
handling systems by both technological means and operations research. Our contribution
lays in the implementation and actual solving of the baggage handling optimization
problem in a new way, using Python/PuLP and GUROBI.
The problem data was described and analyzed based on real data from CPH, in the
form of four days of traffic, adding up to approximately 1200 flights. Along with this, we
investigate the sources of complexity of this case (multiple handlers operating different
equipment that have no real gain in sharing workstations).
In recent years, several researchers have tackled airport management optimization
subjects, many of them with a focus on check-in counter allocation. As a result of this,
multiple new approaches and algorithms are proposed. Having selected from these the
most relevant articles and theses, we then formulated the mathematical model on which
we could build our implementation.
Multiple tests were run on the model, using different restrictions and relaxations to
achieve conclusive results for analysis and proposals. The first scenario was the most
restricted in terms of available allocations, as flights would simply be assigned to dummy
workstations when no correct ones were available. The results showed there is room for
further schedule improvement. Therefore, we began modifying the penalties matrix; this
relaxes the restrictions on possible allocations.
The second scenario permitted allocations of fewer chutes than required, initially
only for flights requiring 3 chutes. Having satisfactory results from this first version, we
also added the possibility of reducing the number of chutes for flights requiring 2 chutes.
Even though this version of scenario gave the lowest chute demand satisfaction rate, it
was still within a very acceptable limit of 95%.
==PAGE:72==
62
8 Conclusion
The test on the third scenario was the only one resulting in 100% chute demand
satisfaction. However, this version permitted allocation of flights in all the available
chutes in BF3, disregarding the handler ownership. At this point, the model confirmed
that flexible chutes are essential for optimal functioning of BF3, considering the current
layout and available technology.
For this reason, the fourth scenario maximizes the
usage of flexible chutes combined with allocation of fewer chutes for each flight. This
last scenario proved to produce the best results (in realistic terms), 97.26% of chute
demand being satisfied without sacrificing the service level.
To better defend the reasoning behind this research paper and check the robustness
of our solution, we also tested two variations of the final scenario with increased traffic
values (10%, respectively 20%). The resulting schedules showed that BF3 is capable
of handling increasing traffic with the current layout, but is nearly at the upper limit
of utilization. However, the rising running times of these last tests also showed that
additional improvements need to be brought to the model. More input from our contact
at CPH could have resulted in scenarios that tackle disruption, as well as more realistic
increased traffic values.
All facts considered, the proposed model illustrated the many advantages to be ob-
tained with a more flexible and data driven approach in chute allocation problems. Test-
ing the different forms of the model revealed many discussion points for further work,
but more data is needed in order to integrate them in a running scenario. We are pleased
with the schedules resulted from all the tests and with the way Python translated the
mathematical model into an adaptive framework that could effortlessly be modified.
